title: Ingestible device with on-board fluorometer and related systems and methods
abstract: An ingestible device includes a sample chamber configured to hold a sample, the sample chamber including a base having an aperture; a fluorometer separated from the sample chamber by the sample chamber base, the fluorometer including (I) a light source configured to emit excitation light, (II) a light guide configured to guide the excitation light from the light source to the sample, (III) a photodetector configured to detect emission light generated via the interaction of the excitation light with the sample. The photodetector is configured to receive the emission light from the sample through the aperture. Additionally, the fluorometer includes (IV) an emission filter between the aperture and the photodetector, the emission filter configured to (i) transmit the emission light incident thereon, and (ii) block excitation light incident thereon. In addition, the ingestible device includes a housing that houses the sample chamber and the fluorometer.
claims: Claims 1. An ingestible device, comprising: a sample chamber configured to hold a sample, the sample chamber comprising a base having an aperture; a fluorometer separated from the sample chamber by the sample chamber base, the fluorometer comprising a light source configured to emit excitation light, a light guide configured to guide the excitation light from the light source to the sample, a photodetector configured to detect emission light generated via the interaction of the excitation light with the sample, wherein the photodetector is configured to receive the emission light from the sample through the aperture, and an emission filter between the aperture and the photodetector, the emission filter configured to (i) transmit the emission light incident thereon, and (ii) block excitation light incident thereon; and a housing houses the sample chamber and the fluorometer. 2. The ingestible device of claim 1, wherein the light guide comprises one or more optical fibers. 3. The ingestible device of claim 2, wherein the light guide comprises an optical -fiber bundle. 4. The ingestible device of claim 1, wherein the sample chamber comprises an assay pad disposed adjacent to the aperture of the sample chamber base and configured to hold the sample. 5. The ingestible device of claim 4, wherein the light guide has an input end coupled with the light source and an output end coupled with the assay pad, the light guide configured to (i) receive the excitation light at the input end, (ii) guide the excitation light from the input end to the output end, and (iii) deliver the excitation light through the output end to the assay pad. 6. The ingestible device of claim 5, wherein the light guide is configured such that the output end is coupled to a surface of the assay pad facing the sample chamber to deliver the excitation light towards the fluorometer. 7. The ingestible device of claim 5, wherein the light guide is configured such that the output end is coupled to a surface of the assay pad facing the fluorometer to deliver the excitation light towards the sample chamber. 8. The ingestible device of claim 1, wherein the light source is disposed on an inner surface of the housing that is (i) part of the fluorometer, and (ii) oriented parallel to the sample- chamber base. 9. The ingestible device of claim 8, wherein the light source is disposed on the same inner surface of the housing as the photodetector. 10. The ingestible device of claim 1, wherein the light source is disposed on an inner surface of the housing that is (i) part of the fluorometer, and (ii) oriented orthogonal to the sample- chamber base. 11. The ingestible device of claim 1, wherein the photodetector is disposed on an inner surface of the housing that is part of the fluorometer at a position where an optical axis of the aperture intersects the inner surface of the housing. 12. The ingestible device of claim 1, wherein inner surfaces of the housing that are part of the fluorometer comprise a light-absorbing material configured to absorb excitation light incident thereon. 13. An ingestible device, comprising: a sample chamber having a base and comprising an assay pad configured to hold a sample, wherein the sample chamber base has an aperture, and the assay pad is disposed adjacent to the aperture; a fluorometer separated from the sample chamber by the sample-chamber base, the fluorometer comprising an annular-shaped light source having an inner edge, the annular-shaped light source configured to emit excitation light at its inner edge, the annular-shaped light source being attached to a fluorometer- side surface of the sample-chamber base to deliver the excitation light to a portion of the assay pad that (i) protrudes through the aperture and (ii) is encircled by the inner edge of the annular-shaped light source, a photodetector configured to detect emission light generated via the interaction of the excitation light with the sample, a fluorescence-collection optic having an input end coupled to the assay pad and an output end coupled with the photodetector, the fluorescence-collection optic configured to (i) receive at the input end emission light emitted by the sample with a first divergence, and (ii) provide, at the output end to the photodetector, the emitted light with a second divergence smaller than the first divergence, and an emission filter between the output end of the fluorescence-collection optic and the photodetector, the emission filter configured to (i) transmit the emission light incident thereon, and (ii) block the excitation light incident thereon; and a housing that houses the sample chamber and the fluorometer. 14. The ingestible device of claim 13, wherein the fluorescence-collection optic is configured as a compound parabolic concentrator (CPC). 15. The ingestible device of claim 14, wherein the CPC is a solid CPC comprising a dielectric material that is transparent to emission light, and a side surface of the CPC is shaped to reflect the emission light received at the input end through total internal reflection (TIR). 16. The ingestible device of claim 14, wherein the CPC is a hollow CPC comprising a reflective material. 17. The ingestible device of claim 13, wherein the emission filter is coupled at the output end of the fluorescence-collection optic, and the photodetector is spaced apart from the emission filter. 18. The ingestible device of any one of claims 13-17, wherein the photodetector is spaced apart from the emission filter by a separation distance in a range of 0-1.5mm. 19. The ingestible device of any one of claims 13-18, wherein the fluorescence-collection optic has a longitudinal size in a range of 5-6mm. 20. An ingestible device, comprising: a sample chamber configured to hold a sample, the sample chamber having a base with an aperture; a fluorometer separated from the sample chamber by the sample-chamber base, the fluorometer comprising a light source configured to emit excitation light, a photodetector configured to detect emission light generated via the interaction of the excitation light with sample, wherein the photodetector is configured to receive the emission light from the sample through the aperture, and an emission filter between the aperture and the photodetector, the emission filter configured to (i) transmit the emission light incident thereon, and (ii) block the excitation light incident thereon; and a housing that houses the sample chamber and the fluorometer, wherein inner surfaces of the housing that are part of the fluorometer comprise a light-absorbing material configured to absorb excitation light incident thereon. 21. The ingestible device of claim 20, wherein a fluorometer-facing surface of the sample- chamber base comprises the same or another light-absorbing material as the one in the light absorbing material of the inner surfaces of the housing configured to absorb excitation light incident thereon. 22. The ingestible device of claim 20, wherein the aperture is a window comprising a material that is transparent to both excitation light and emission light, and the window is coated on one or both of its surfaces with coating that is antireflective for both the excitation light and the emission light incident thereon. 23. The ingestible device of claim 20, wherein the sample chamber comprises an assay pad disposed adjacent to the aperture of the sample chamber base and configured to hold the sample. 24. The ingestible device of claim 23, wherein the assay pad comprises a sponge. 25. The ingestible device of claim 24, wherein an internal structure of the sponge is configured to forward scattered the excitation light received thereon from the light source. 26. The ingestible device of claim 25, wherein the sponge comprises light-absorbing backing configured to absorb the forward scattered excitation light incident thereon. 27. The ingestible device of claim 20, wherein the fluorometer further comprises relay optics between the light source and the assay pad, and configured to image the light source onto the assay pad. 28. The ingestible device of any one of the claims 20-27, wherein the fluorometer further comprises an excitation filter between the light source and aperture, the excitation filter configured to transmit a portion of the excitation light. 29. The ingestible device of any claims 20-28, wherein the light source and the photodetector are configured symmetrically with respect to an optical axis of the aperture in a plane that includes the optical axis, the light sourceâs location and the photodetectorâs location. 30. The ingestible device of claim 28, wherein a portion of the housing is inside the fluorometer between the light source and the photodetector, and the ingestible device comprises a separator inside the fluorometer and extending between a first end of the separator adjacent to the portion of the housing disposed between the light source and the photodetector and a second end of the separator adjacent to the aperture of the sample-chamber base, where a first combination of (i) a first side of the separator, (ii) a first portion of the housing that supports the excitation filter, and (iii) a first portion of the sample-chamber base defines a first enclosure of the fluorometer, a second combination of (i) a second side of the separator opposing the first side of the separator, (ii) a second portion of the housing that supports the emission filter, and (iii) a second portion of the sample-chamber base defines a second enclosure of the fluorometer, and the first enclosure is configured to impede excitation light scattered therein from reaching the photodetector without first interacting with the sample and then propagating through the second enclosure. 31. The ingestible device of claim 30, wherein the separator comprises a plate. 32. The ingestible device of claim 30, wherein the housing comprises the separator. 33. The ingestible device of claim 30, wherein the first and second sides of the separator comprise the same or another light-absorbing material as the one in the light-absorbing material of the inner surfaces of the housing configured to absorb excitation light incident thereon. 34. The ingestible device of claim 20 or claim 30, wherein a first aperture adjacent to the excitation filter defines an excitation path along which excitation light propagates through the first enclosure from the excitation filter to the aperture of the sample-chamber base without scattering off the housing, the sample-chamber base, or the first side of the separator, and a second aperture adjacent to the emission filter defines an emission path along which emission light propagates through the second enclosure from the aperture of the sample- chamber base to the excitation filter without scattering off the housing, the sample-chamber base, or the second side of the separator. 35. The ingestible device of claim 34, wherein the housing comprises a shelf that has two openings corresponding to the first and second apertures, and the shelf is configured to support the excitation filter adjacent to the first aperture and the emission filter adjacent to the second aperture. 36. The ingestible device of claim 34, wherein the fluorometer comprises a first plate disposed adjacent to the excitation filter and having an opening corresponding to the first aperture, and a second plate disposed adjacent to the emission filter and having an opening corresponding to the second aperture. 37. The ingestible device of any one of claims 34-36, wherein a pair of apertures adjacent to the aperture of the sample-chamber base is configured to reinforce the definition of the corresponding excitation path and emission path, wherein the pair of apertures has two openings that are disposed on separate sides of the separator. 38. The ingestible device of claim 37, wherein the openings of the pair of apertures are circular. 39. The ingestible device of claim 37, wherein the openings of the pair of apertures are shaped as segments of a circle. 40. The ingestible device of claim 30, wherein the first portion of the sample-chamber base has a smaller lateral extent from the separator than the aperture of the sample-chamber base, such that the first enclosure is shaped as a first tunnel path, and the second portion of the sample-chamber base has a smaller lateral extent from the separator than the aperture of the sample-chamber base, such that the second enclosure is shaped as a second tunnel path. 41. The ingestible device of claim 40, comprises a first sequence of baffles which protrude into the first tunnel path from (i) a first series of locations of the first side of the separator, distributed from its first end adjacent to the excitation filter to its second end adjacent to the aperture of the sample-chamber base, and (ii) corresponding locations of the housing, where the first sequence of baffles defines a first sequence of respective apertures that collectively define an excitation light path between the light source and the aperture of the sample-chamber base, and a second sequence of baffles which protrude into the second tunnel path from (i) a second series of locations of the second side of the separator, distributed from its second end adjacent to the aperture of the sample-chamber base to the emission filter, and (ii) corresponding locations of the housing, where the second sequence of baffles defines a second sequence of respective apertures that collectively define an emission light path between the aperture of the sample-chamber base and the photodetector. 42. The ingestible device of claim 41, wherein the first and second sequences of baffles comprise the same or another light-absorbing material as the one in the light-absorbing material of the inner surfaces of the housing and the first and second sides of the separator configured to absorb excitation light incident thereon. 43. The ingestible device of claim 20, wherein the light source is in a first plane that includes an optical axis of the aperture, the photodetector is arranged in a second plane that includes the optical axis, the second plane being different from the first plane, the aperture is a window comprising a material that is transparent to both excitation light and emission light, and the fluorometer further comprises a beam dump in the first plane and configured to absorb excitation light specularly reflected from the window. 44. An ingestible device, comprising: a sample chamber configured to hold a sample, the sample chamber comprising a base having an aperture; a fluorometer separated from the sample chamber by the sample chamber base, the fluorometer comprising a light source configured to emit excitation light, an excitation filter between the light source and aperture, the excitation filter configured to transmit a portion of the excitation light, a first light guide configured to guide excitation light transmitted through the excitation filter to the sample, a photodetector configured to detect emission light generated via the interaction of the excitation light with the sample, an emission filter between the aperture and the photodetector, the emission filter configured to (i) transmit the emission light incident thereon, and (ii) block excitation light incident thereon, and a second light guide configured receive the excitation light through the aperture and to guide the received excitation light to the emission filter; and a housing that houses the sample chamber and the fluorometer. 45. The ingestible device of claim 44, wherein each of the first and second light guide comprises one or more optical fibers. 46. The ingestible device of claim 45, wherein either the first light guide or the second light guide or both comprises an optical-fiber bundle. 47. The ingestible device of claim 44, wherein the sample chamber comprises an assay pad disposed adjacent to the aperture of the sample chamber base and configured to hold the sample. 48. The ingestible device of claim 47, wherein the first light guide has an input end coupled with the excitation filter and an output end coupled with the assay pad, the first light guide configured to (i) receive the excitation light at the input end, (ii) guide the excitation light from the input end to the output end, and (iii) deliver the excitation light through the output end to the assay pad, and the second light guide has an input end coupled with the assay pad and an output end coupled with the emission filter, the second light guide configured to (i) receive the emission light at the input end, (ii) guide the emission light from the input end to the output end, and (iii) deliver the emission light through the output end to the emission filter. 49. The ingestible device of claim 44, wherein the light source is disposed on the same inner surface of the housing as the photodetector. 50. The ingestible device of claim 44, wherein inner surfaces of the housing that are part of the fluorometer comprise a light-absorbing material configured to absorb excitation light incident thereon. 51. The ingestible device of any one of the preceding claims, wherein the fluorometer has a longitudinal size in a range of 6-10mm and a transverse size in a range of 8-13mm. 52. The ingestible device of any one of the preceding claims, wherein the ingestible device has a longitudinal size in a range of 20-30mm and a transverse size in a range of 8-13mm. 53. The ingestible device of any one of the preceding claims, further comprising a sampling subsystem configured to retrieve the sample from the gastrointestinal (GI) tract of a subject in vivo. 54. The ingestible device of any one of the preceding claims, wherein the light source comprises one or more light emitting diodes. 55. The ingestible device of any one of the preceding claims, wherein the photodetector comprises one or more photodiodes or photomultipliers. 56. The ingestible device of any one of the preceding claims, further comprising a power subsystem housed within the housing and configured to provide power to the light source and the photodetector. 57. The ingestible device of any one of the preceding claims, further comprising an electronics subsystem housed within the housing and configured to synchronize activation of the light source and readout of the photodetector. 58. The ingestible device of any one of the preceding claims, wherein the emission filter is a long- wavelength pass filter having a lower-bound wavelength shorter than wavelengths of the emission light. 59. The ingestible device of claim 58, wherein the excitation filter is a short- wavelength pass filter having an upper-bound wavelength longer than wavelengths of the excitation light, and shorter than the lower-bound wavelength of the emission filter. 60. The ingestible device of any one of the preceding claims, wherein the assay pad comprises absorbent material. 61. The ingestible device of claim 60, wherein the absorbent material comprises a sponge. 62. A system comprising: the ingestible device of any one of the previous claims; and a hardware processor configured to produce information about the sample based on characteristics of the detected emission light.
